---
title: "Blub & Fuel"
subtitle: "Five engines, one domain — a junior C# walk up the language ladder"
---

# Welcome {.unnumbered}

This is a teaching book.

It exists because a senior engineer can tell you "F# is better at this" and
a junior will nod, but neither of them will know *what kind of bug* the
junior was about to ship that F# would have refused to compile. Paul
Graham calls this the **blub paradox** — you can't see what your current
language is missing until you've used a more expressive one.

So instead of one essay, we built the same small domain — a fuel-upload
engine that parses transactions, validates them, decides duplicates,
quarantines suspicious ones, and emits a summary — **five times**, in
five different ways:

| Engine                  | Style                                                    |
| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| `normalcsharp-fuel-engine` | C# the way a junior writes it: mutable POCOs, string-typed status, exceptions as control flow, no NRT |
| `csharp-fuel-engine`    | C# the way a senior writes it: sealed-record DUs, NRT, switch expressions, `IReadOnlyList`, records |
| `fsharp-fuel-engine`    | F# — native discriminated unions, exhaustive pattern matching, immutability by default |
| `haskell-fuel-engine`   | Haskell — type-driven everything: `NonEmpty`, `data`, purity, `IO` quarantine |
| `rust-fuel-engine`      | Rust — same algebraic types as Haskell, plus ownership and zero-cost abstractions |

All five produce the same outputs for the same inputs. They differ in
**which class of bug each one even allows you to make**.

## How to read this book

There are two ways.

**Part I — The Journey** is the linear narrative. You start at the normal-C#
implementation, you walk up the ladder one rung at a time, and you watch
each language make a class of error structurally impossible. Ten chapters,
each 5–10 minutes. If you read end to end, that's the blub paradox
delivered in one sitting.

**Part II — The Reference** is the lookup. You pick a topic (modelling the
decision, validation, recovery policy, exhaustiveness, mutability, null,
boundary mapping) and see how all five languages handle it side-by-side,
with tradeoffs called out. Each reference chapter is 1–3 pages.

The two halves are cross-linked. Part I points into Part II when you'd
benefit from a deeper side-by-side; Part II points back into Part I for the
narrative context.

## What you'll get out of it

- A vocabulary for things your current language doesn't model — *exhaustive
  pattern matching*, *non-null-by-default*, *discriminated unions*, *type-level
  guarantees on collections*, *no hidden side effects*.
- A clear picture of which of those each of these languages gives you for
  free, which you have to opt into, and which require leaving the language.
- A taxonomy of seven concrete footguns in junior C# code, mapped to the
  language feature that would have closed each one.
- An answer to "how easy is this to extend?" for each language. You'll see
  what happens when you add a 7th case to a discriminated union in each one.

## Source code

All five implementations are checked in alongside this book. Read [`Setup`](setup.qmd)
for how to build and run them locally.
